metadata.title: Pharmacokinetics of Fluconazole in Saliva and Plasma after Administration of an Oral Suspension and Capsules
metadata.abstract: C. H. W. KOKS,[1] * P. L. MEENHORST,[2] M. J. X. HILLEBRAND,[1] A. BULT,[3] AND J. H. BEIJNEN[1,3] _Department of Pharmacy_[1] _and Department of Internal Medicine,_[2] _Slotervaart Hospital, 1066 EC Amsterdam, and Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis and Toxicology, Utrecht University, 3508 TB Utrecht,_[3] _The Netherlands_

evidence.original_text: Fluconazole is an orally active bistriazole antifungal agent which is used in the treatment of superficial and systemic candidiasis and in the treatment of cryptococcal infections in patients with AIDS.

Flushing and swallowing of the oral suspension resulted in a significantly (P ≤ 0.005) higher mean area under the concentration-versus-time curve (AUC) from 0 to 24 h in saliva (89.13 ± 23.42 mg·h/liter) than that obtained after ingestion of the same dose as capsules (69.27 ± 12.89 mg·h/liter).
The fluconazole oral suspension and capsules resulted in comparable concentrations and AUCs in plasma.

Thus, because of a higher local level of drug exposure in terms of both higher peak concentrations in saliva and a higher salivary AUC, the fluconazole oral suspension has theoretical advantages over the capsule formulation in the treatment of oropharyngeal candidiases.

Flushing and swallowing of the oral suspension resulted in a significantly (P ≤ 0.005) higher mean area under the concentration-versus-time curve (AUC) from 0 to 24 h in saliva (89.13 ± 23.42 mg·h/liter) than that obtained after ingestion of the same dose as capsules (69.27 ± 12.89 mg·h/liter).

The calculated mean maximum concentration in saliva just after swallowing of the suspension was 97.99 ± 6.12 mg/liter. The peak fluconazole concentration in saliva after the ingestion of the capsules was 3.55 ± 0.40 mg/liter.
Saliva and plasma samples were analyzed by a validated high-performance liquid chromatographic assay.

The concentrations of fluconazole were determined at steady state in the saliva and plasma of 10 healthy volunteers after ingestion of fluconazole as capsules and after flushing the mouth for 2 min with the same dose formulated as an oral suspension and swallowing of the drug.
Fluconazole has a high degree (≥90%) of bioavailability after oral administration, displays pH-independent absorption, and is eliminated, predominantly unchanged, by renal excretion.
As a result of its beneficial lipophilic/hydrophilic profile and its low level (12%) of binding to plasma proteins, fluconazole readily penetrates into body tissue.
Concomitant with its widespread use, the number of reports of fluconazole resistance, mainly in patients with advanced AIDS, is increasing (1, 7).
One of the described mechanisms of this resistance is a reduced permeability of the yeast cell (4).

Because drug passage through the yeast cell wall is a passive, concentration-driven process (diffusion) which can theoretically be overcome by exposure to higher drug concentrations, we investigated whether the recently introduced oral suspension of fluconazole might have advantages compared with the capsule formulation in terms of (sustained) higher peak concentrations in saliva and higher local levels of drug exposure, expressed as the salivary fluconazole AUC.

Ten healthy volunteers (nine men and one woman) were recruited for the study, which was approved by the Medical Ethical and Trial Review Boards of the Slotervaart Hospital, Amsterdam, The Netherlands.

In order to achieve rapidly concentrations comparable to those achieved in a steady-state situation of a daily intake of 100 mg of fluconazole, the participants were instructed to ingest 200 mg of fluconazole on day 1 and 100 mg on day 2 (on both days as capsules).

Stimulated saliva samples were collected by chewing for 30 to 45 s on the cotton wool swabs of a saliva-collecting device (Salivette; Sarstedt, Nümbrecht, Germany) impregnated with 20 mg of citric acid immediately before and at 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 24 h after fluconazole ingestion.
All pharmacokinetic calculations were performed with the computer package MW/PHARM (version 3.02; Mediware, Groningen, The Netherlands).
Differences in individual AUC0–24s for both saliva and plasma after ingestion of the fluconazole capsules and the suspension were statistically tested by a paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test.
Topical application of antifungal agents (e.g., clotrimazole troches, nystatin, or amphotericin B lozenges or oral suspension) is traditionally used in the treatment of oral candidiasis.

If, for example, the peak concentration/MIC ratio or AUC/MIC ratio are linked, the suspension should be better. If the time above the MIC is the determining factor, the two preparations will be equally effective.

Flushing and swallowing of the oral suspension resulted in a significantly (P ≤ 0.005) higher mean area under the concentration-versus-time curve (AUC) from 0 to 24 h in saliva (89.13 ± 23.42 mg·h/liter) than that obtained after ingestion of the same dose as capsules (69.27 ± 12.89 mg·h/liter).

evidence.original_text: The profiles of the mean concentrations of fluconazole in saliva after ingestion of the same dose of fluconazole as capsules and as suspension are depicted in Fig. 1. The mean measured Cmax in saliva after ingestion of the fluconazole capsules was 3.55 ± 0.40 mg/liter 3 h after ingestion. The mean Cmax in saliva, which is the concentration directly after swallowing the suspension, was calculated by backextrapolation of the fitted curve to be 97.99 ± 6.12 mg/liter. The mean fluconazole concentrations in saliva on day 3 and day 4 (prior to the daily drug administration) were 2.13 ± 0.78 and 2.25 ± 0.60 mg/liter, respectively.

Plasma and saliva fluconazole levels were measured by a validated HPLC method (5). Briefly, an aliquot of plasma was mixed with the internal standard (phenacetin) and was extracted with chloroform–1-propanol (4:1; vol/vol). After evaporation of the organic extract, the residue was redissolved in the mobile phase. Saliva samples were treated in the same way (but without the internal standard). Chromatographic analysis was performed on a Lichrosorb RP-18 (5-μm) column by isocratic elution with a mobile phase of 0.01 M acetate buffer (pH 5.0)–methanol (70:30; vol/vol) and UV detection at 261 nm.

All pharmacokinetic calculations were performed with the computer package MW/PHARM (version 3.02; Mediware, Groningen, The Netherlands). This is a nonlinear curve-fitting program based on the least-squares method (9). The pharmacokinetic profile of the fluconazole concentrations in saliva after ingestion of the suspension could best be described with an open two-compartment model. The pharmacokinetic profiles of the plasma fluconazole concentrations (from both capsules and suspension) and the concentrations in saliva after use of the capsules could be adequately described with an open one-compartment model.

Differences in individual AUC0–24s for both saliva and plasma after ingestion of the fluconazole capsules and the suspension were statistically tested by a paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test. P values of less than 0.05 were considered significant.

Ten healthy volunteers (nine men and one woman) were recruited for the study, which was approved by the Medical Ethical and Trial Review Boards of the Slotervaart Hospital, Amsterdam, The Netherlands. The volunteers were 21 to 38 years old (median age, 29 years) and weighed 63 to 82 kg (median weight, 70 kg). In order to achieve rapidly concentrations comparable to those achieved in a steady-state situation of a daily intake of 100 mg of fluconazole, the participants were instructed to ingest 200 mg of fluconazole on day 1 and 100 mg on day 2 (on both days as capsules). On day 3 100 mg of fluconazole was administered as an oral suspension (5 ml = 100 mg). The participants were instructed to flush the suspension through the oral cavity for 2 min and subsequently to swallow it.

Venous blood samples were drawn into heparinized tubes immediately before and at 3, 5, and 24 h after drug ingestion. Stimulated saliva samples were collected by chewing for 30 to 45 s on the cotton wool swabs of a saliva-collecting device (Salivette; Sarstedt, Nümbrecht, Germany) impregnated with 20 mg of citric acid immediately before and at 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 24 h after fluconazole ingestion. Plasma and saliva were obtained by centrifuging the blood samples and cotton wool swabs, respectively. The samples were stored at −30°C prior to analysis.

Statistical analysis of individual data revealed that after intake of the fluconazole suspension, the salivary AUC0–24 was significantly (P ≤ 0.005) higher than that after ingestion of the capsules. There was no statistically significant difference (P = 0.953) in the plasma AUC0–24 after ingestion of the fluconazole suspension or the capsules.

The mean measured Cmax in saliva after ingestion of the fluconazole capsules was 3.55 ± 0.40 mg/liter 3 h after ingestion. The mean Cmax in saliva, which is the concentration directly after swallowing the suspension, was calculated by backextrapolation of the fitted curve to be 97.99 ± 6.12 mg/liter.

Thus, because of a higher local level of drug exposure in terms of both higher peak concentrations in saliva and a higher salivary AUC, the fluconazole oral suspension has theoretical advantages over the capsule formulation in the treatment of oropharyngeal candidiases.

Because drug passage through the yeast cell wall is a passive, concentration-driven process (diffusion) which can theoretically be overcome by exposure to higher drug concentrations, we investigated whether the recently introduced oral suspension of fluconazole might have advantages compared with the capsule formulation in terms of (sustained) higher peak concentrations in saliva and higher local levels of drug exposure, expressed as the salivary fluconazole AUC.

Thus, because of a higher local level of drug exposure in terms of both higher peak concentrations in saliva and a higher salivary AUC, the fluconazole oral suspension has theoretical advantages over the capsule formulation in the treatment of oropharyngeal candidiases.

There are, however, no data that link a specific aspect of the plasma or saliva concentration-time curve to therapeutic outcomes for triazoles. If, for example, the peak concentration/MIC ratio or AUC/MIC ratio are linked, the suspension should be better. If the time above the MIC is the determining factor, the two preparations will be equally effective.
Whether the fluconazole suspension has advantages over the capsules in the treatment of oropharyngeal candidiasis is being tested.
